items.q: Does normalisation mean deleting data from the database?
items.correct: No - normalisation restructures tables so each fact is stored once; no information is lost, only redundant copies are removed.
items.misconception: Yes - normalising a database throws away records to save space.
items.random_wrong: Yes - normalisation compresses the data so it cannot be queried.
items.why: Removing redundancy sounds like removing data. Decomposing a table into linked tables preserves everything - joins can rebuild the original exactly.

items.q: Does NULL in a field mean the value zero?
items.correct: No - NULL means the value is absent or unknown, which is different from zero or an empty string, and it behaves differently in comparisons.
items.misconception: Yes - NULL is just how databases display the number zero.
items.random_wrong: Yes - NULL means the row has been deleted.
items.why: Zero is a value; NULL is the lack of one. The distinction matters in aggregates and WHERE clauses, where NULL comparisons return unknown rather than true or false.

items.q: Must a foreign key value be unique within its table?
items.correct: No - foreign keys can repeat; many orders may reference the same customer. It is the primary key in the referenced table that must be unique.
items.misconception: Yes - all key columns of any kind must contain unique values.
items.random_wrong: Yes - duplicate foreign keys break the database engine.
items.why: The uniqueness rule of primary keys gets over-generalised. Repeating foreign key values is precisely how the 'many' side of a one-to-many relationship works.

items.q: The main purpose of normalisation is to:
items.correct: Eliminate data redundancy and the anomalies it causes
items.misconception: Make every table as wide as possible
items.random_wrong: Encrypt the data in each table
items.why: It's Eliminate data redundancy and the anomalies it causes. “Make every table as wide as possible” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A foreign key is used to:
items.correct: Implement a relationship by referencing another table's primary key
items.misconception: Uniquely identify rows in its own table in all cases
items.why: It's Implement a relationship by referencing another table's primary key. “Uniquely identify rows in its own table in all cases” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which SQL statement retrieves data from a database?
items.why: It's SELECT. “FETCHALL” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A table in third normal form must have:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: No dependencies between non-key attributes. “At least ten columns” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: To implement a many-to-many relationship between two tables, how many additional link (junction) tables are needed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 table. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
